rationale: This reaction is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction, demonstrating the formation of a stable tert-butyl carbocation from tert-butanol under strong acid (sulfuric acid) catalysis. This carbocation is then trapped by a simple nitrile, acetonitrile, which after hydrolysis yields the corresponding N-tert-butylacetamide. All components are simple, acyclic, and clearly illustrate the core transformation of an alcohol and a nitrile into an N-substituted amide.
collision_avoidance_note: The use of simple, acyclic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) avoids complex ring systems or functional groups that could overlap with other named reactions. It specifically avoids arenes (Friedel-Crafts), alkenes (hydroamination/Markovnikov additions), or other potentially reactive motifs, focusing purely on the carbocation-nitrile coupling characteristic of the Ritter reaction.
